perturbation.original_text: During the Restricted Period, Covenantor shall not directly or indirectly (i) solicit or encourage any employee of Buyer to leave the employ of Buyer or (ii) hire any employee who has left the employ of Buyer if such hiring is proposed to occur within one year after the termination of such employee's employment with Buyer.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Restricted Period, Covenantor shall not directly or indirectly (i) solicit or encourage any employee of Buyer to leave the employ of Buyer or (ii) hire any employee who has left the employ of Buyer if such hiring is proposed to occur within six months after the termination of such employee's employment with Buyer.
perturbation.explanation: The modification reduces the no-hire period to six months, which may be considered an unreasonably short duration under California law, particularly given the context of protecting trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While this section is about non-competes and not necessarily non-solicitation or no-hire agreements, California courts scrutinize such agreements to ensure they don't effectively function as non-competes. A six-month restriction on hiring former employees, in some cases, may be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade if it is longer than necessary to protect the employer's legitimate interests, such as trade secrets, especially if the employee's role is not highly specialized. Six months may not be long enough to protect the confidential information, and the protection may be challenged.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the no-hire period from one year to six months. The Law Explanation suggests this shorter period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially creating an unreasonable restraint on trade under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," unless an exception applies. While the agreement isn't a complete non-compete, California courts scrutinize such agreements to ensure they don't effectively function as non-competes. The change in the no-hire period creates a legal contradiction as the reduced duration could be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade.

perturbation.original_text: Covenantor shall not at any time within the period commencing on the date hereof and ending on the fourth (4/th/) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), have any -2- <PAGE> ownership interest (of record or beneficial) in or have any interest as an employee, salesman, consultant, officer or director in, or otherwise aid or assist in any manner, any firm, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, proprietorship or other business that engages in any California county, or in any other state, country or geographic area, listed in Exhibit "A" hereof in a business that is similar to and directly or indirectly offers products or services in competition with the business in which the Company was engaged in such California county, state, country or other geographic area as of the Closing, so long as Buyer, or any successor in interest of Buyer to the business and goodwill of the Company, remains engaged in such business in any such California county, state, country or other geographic area or continues to solicit customers or potential customers therein
perturbation.changed_text: Covenantor shall not at any time within the period commencing on the date hereof and ending on the fifth (5/th/) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), have any -2- <PAGE> ownership interest (of record or beneficial) in or have any interest as an employee, salesman, consultant, officer or director in, or otherwise aid or assist in any manner, any firm, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, proprietorship or other business that engages in any California county, or in any other state, country or geographic area, listed in Exhibit "A" hereof in a business that is similar to and directly or indirectly offers products or services in competition with the business in which the Company was engaged in such California county, state, country or other geographic area as of the Closing, so long as Buyer, or any successor in interest of Buyer to the business and goodwill of the Company, remains engaged in such business in any such California county, state, country or other geographic area or continues to solicit customers or potential customers therein
perturbation.explanation: The modification extends the non-compete period to five years, which may be considered unreasonably long under California law, making the non-compete agreement more likely to be deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While the reasonableness of a non-compete's duration depends on the specific facts, a five-year restriction is a significant restraint on trade and is more susceptible to being deemed overly broad and unenforceable under California law, especially if it is not narrowly tailored to protect trade secrets.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from four to five years. The Perturbation Explanation states that this extension may be considered unreasonably long under California law. The Law Explanation states that a five-year restriction is more susceptible to being deemed overly broad and unenforceable under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The extension of the non-compete period increases the restraint on trade, making it more likely to be void under this law.

perturbation.original_text: 12.4 Notices. All notices, requests and other communications hereunder ------- shall be in writing and shall be delivered by courier or other means of personal service (including by means of a nationally recognized courier service or professional messenger service), or sent by facsimile or mailed first class, postage prepaid, by certified mail, return receipt requested, in all cases, addressed to:
perturbation.changed_text: 12.4 Notices. All notices, requests and other communications hereunder ------- shall be in writing and shall be delivered only by regular mail, postage prepaid, addressed to:
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the options for delivering notices via courier, personal service, or certified mail, restricting it solely to regular mail, which may be insufficient for legally required notices, particularly those needing proof of delivery. This contradicts the requirements for notice in certain legal contexts, particularly those related to wage claims or other employment disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1013
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1013 specifies rules for service by mail, including extending response times when service is by mail within California. While not explicitly mandating specific methods, it assumes and regulates mail as a means of legal service. Eliminating options like certified mail and personal service could be problematic if legal requirements mandate proof of delivery, as regular mail does not provide that, which could violate due process requirements depending on the context.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The changed text restricts notice delivery to regular mail, eliminating options like courier, personal service, and certified mail which are standard for legally sensitive communications. The Law Explanation specifies that eliminating certified mail and personal service could be problematic if proof of delivery is mandated, a common requirement for serving legal notices. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1013(a), discusses 'service by mail,' implying its acceptance as a method but does not explicitly mandate it as the sole acceptable method. However, the Perturbation Explanation states that using only regular mail may be insufficient for legally required notices, especially where proof of delivery is needed. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction by potentially failing to meet legally required proof of delivery standards, contradicting the implicit requirements for proper legal service.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE 7 OWNERSHIP AND RETURN OF MATERIALS --------------------------------- Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer, Covenantor shall immediately surrender to Buyer all notes, data, sketches, drawings, manuals, documents, records, data bases, programs, blueprints, memoranda, specifications, customer lists, financial reports, equipment and all other physical forms of expression incorporating or containing any Confidential Information, it being distinctly understood that all such writings, physical forms of expression and other things are the exclusive property of Buyer. Covenantor acknowledges that the unauthorized taking of any of Buyer's trade secrets is a crime under California Penal Code section 499(c) and is punishable by imprisonment in a state prison or in a county jail for a time not exceeding one year, or by a fine not exceeding five thousand dollars ($5,000), or by both such fine and such imprisonment.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE 7 OWNERSHIP AND RETURN OF MATERIALS --------------------------------- Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer, Covenantor shall surrender to Buyer all notes, data, sketches, drawings, manuals, documents, records, data bases, programs, blueprints, memoranda, specifications, customer lists, financial reports, equipment and all other physical forms of expression incorporating or containing any Confidential Information within 90 days of termination. Covenantor acknowledges that the unauthorized taking of any of Buyer's trade secrets is a crime under California Penal Code section 499(c) and is punishable by imprisonment in a state prison or in a county jail for a time not exceeding one year, or by a fine not exceeding five thousand dollars ($5,000), or by both such fine and such imprisonment.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return timeframe to "within 90 days of termination" contradicts the requirement of immediate return upon termination. This delay could impede the employer's ability to protect its trade secrets and confidential information effectively, and can cause damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Delaying the return of confidential materials by 90 days increases the risk of misappropriation or misuse of those secrets, undermining the purpose of CUTSA. Immediate return is implicitly necessary for preventing potential damage.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day delay for returning confidential information, directly contradicting the original contract's requirement for immediate surrender upon termination. This delay increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, undermining the protections afforded by trade secret law, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The original text aligns with the protection of trade secrets by mandating immediate return. The modified text weakens this protection by allowing a delay, which could facilitate the misuse or disclosure of confidential information, creating a significant legal contradiction under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any action, suit, or other proceeding concerning the negotiation, interpretation, validity, performance, or breach of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall recover all of such party's reasonable attorneys' fees, expenses, and costs, not limited to costs of suit, incurred in each and every such action, suit, or other proceeding, including any and all appeals or petitions relating thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any action, suit, or other proceeding concerning the negotiation, interpretation, validity, performance, or breach of this Agreement, neither party shall recover attorneys' fees, expenses, and costs, regardless of which party prevails.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the prevailing party's right to recover attorneys' fees. This could be problematic because certain claims, particularly those related to wage disputes under California law, provide a statutory right to attorneys' fees to a prevailing employee.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 218.5(a) states that in any action brought for the nonpayment of wages, fringe benefits, or health and welfare or pension fund contributions, the court shall award reasonable attorney's fees and costs to the prevailing party if any party requests attorney's fees and costs upon the initiation of the action. By removing the possibility of recovering attorney fees in the agreement, the contract contradicts this section of the California Labor Code.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 218.5. (a) In any action brought for the nonpayment of wages, fringe benefits, or health and welfare or pension fund contributions, the court shall award reasonable attorney’s fees and costs to the prevailing party if any party to the action requests attorney’s fees and costs upon the initiation of the action. However, if the prevailing party in the court action is not an employee, attorney’s fees and costs shall be awarded pursuant to this section only if the court finds that the employee brought the court action in bad faith. This section shall not apply to an action brought by the Labor Commissioner. This section shall not apply to a surety issuing a bond pursuant to Chapter 9 (commencing with Section 7000) of Division 3 of the Business and Professions Code or to an action to enforce a mechanics lien brought under Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 8400) of Title 2 of Part 6 of Division 4 of the Civil
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly removes the prevailing party's right to recover attorney's fees, expenses, and costs, regardless of who prevails. This directly contradicts California Labor Code Section 218.5(a), which mandates that in any action for the nonpayment of wages, fringe benefits, or health and welfare or pension fund contributions, the court 'shall award reasonable attorney’s fees and costs to the prevailing party if any party to the action requests attorney’s fees and costs upon the initiation of the action.' The original text allowed for the prevailing party to recover these fees. The modification introduces a clear legal contradiction by removing a right to attorney's fees that is legally mandated under specific circumstances.
